plainMeaning: Naming all eighteen kinds of wrongdoing one by one, the reciter admits to any he may have done, had done, or approved of, and asks that every such fault be wiped away.
recitation: pahele prāṇātipāta, bīje mṛṣāvāda, trīje adattādāna, cothe maithuna, pāṁcame parigraha, chaṭṭhe krodha, sātame māna, āṭhame māyā, navame lobha, daśame rāga, agiyārame dveṣa, bārame kalaha, terame abhyākhyāna, caudame cāḍī, paṁdarame rati-arati, soḷame para-parivāda, sattarame māyā-mṛṣāvāda, aḍhārame mithyātva-śalya, e aḍhāre pāpasthānaka-māṁhe jiko koi pāpasthānaka māre jīve sevyuṁ hoya, sevarāvyuṁ hoya, sevatāṁ pratye anumodyuṁ hoya, te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ordinal locative of pahelo 'first', from Skt. prathama via Prakrit paḍhama.
wordByWord.notes: Sets the counting frame: each item is fixed by its serial number.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. ordinal of bījo 'second', from Skt. dvitīya.
Guj. ordinal of trījo, from Skt. tṛtīya.

wordByWord.gloss: taking what is not given; theft (item 3)

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. ordinal of cotho, from Skt. caturtha.
Guj. ordinal of pāṁcamo, from Skt. pañcama.
Guj. ordinal of chaṭṭho, from Skt. ṣaṣṭha.

wordByWord.gloss: anger (item 6; first of the four kaṣāyas)

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. ordinal of sātamo, from Skt. saptama.
Guj. ordinal of āṭhamo, from Skt. aṣṭama.
Guj. ordinal of navamo, from Skt. navama.

wordByWord.gloss: greed (item 9; last of the four kaṣāyas)

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. ordinal of daśamo, from Skt. daśama.
Guj. ordinal of agiyāramo, from Skt. ekādaśa.
Guj. ordinal of bāramo, from Skt. dvādaśa.
Guj. ordinal of teramo, from Skt. trayodaśa.
Guj. ordinal of caudamo, from Skt. caturdaśa.
Deśya/Guj. cāḍī 'informing, slander'; classical equivalent paiśunya.
Guj. ordinal of paṁdaramo, from Skt. pañcadaśa.

wordByWord.gloss: delight (in the wrong) and discontent (with the right) (item 15)

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. ordinal of soḷamo, from Skt. ṣoḍaśa.
Guj. ordinal of sattaramo, from Skt. saptadaśa.
Guj. ordinal of aḍhāramo, from Skt. aṣṭādaśa.
Skt. mithyātva 'wrongness of view' + śalya 'thorn, dart'.
Guj. demonstrative e 'this/these', from Skt. eta-.
pāpasthānaka + Guj. postposition māṁhe 'within, among' (from Skt. madhye).
Old Guj. relative jiko 'whoever/whichever', from Skt. ya-.

wordByWord.gloss: by my / by me (with jīve: 'by my soul')

wordByWord.notes: māre jīve = 'by my own soul' — the reciter's soul is the locus of confession.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. past participle of sevvuṁ, from Skt. √sev 'to serve, practise'.

wordByWord.gloss: may be (the case); if it has been
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. subjunctive of thavuṁ/hovuṁ 'to be', from Skt. √bhū.
wordByWord.notes: First of three occurrences — pairs with sevyuṁ (doing oneself).

wordByWord.gloss: has been caused to be committed (by another; kārita)
wordByWord.etymology: Causative of sevvuṁ + Guj. past participle ending.

wordByWord.notes: Second occurrence — pairs with sevarāvyuṁ (causing another).
sevatāṁ pratye = 'toward those committing it' — sets up approval of another's act.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. anumodana 'approval' + Guj. past participle ending; √mud 'to rejoice'.

wordByWord.notes: Third occurrence — pairs with anumodyuṁ (approving another), completing the triple agency.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. first-person pronoun, from Skt. aham via Prakrit huṁ.
Guj. absolutive of karvuṁ 'to do', from Skt. √kṛ.
Prakrit micchā, from Skt. mithyā 'falsely, fruitlessly'.

wordByWord.notes: micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ = 'may my misdeed come to nothing' — the sealing retraction.

literalTranslation: At the first, injury to life; at the second, false speech; at the third, taking what is not given; at the fourth, unchastity; at the fifth, possessiveness; at the sixth, anger; at the seventh, pride; at the eighth, deceit; at the ninth, greed; at the tenth, attachment; at the eleventh, aversion; at the twelfth, quarrelling; at the thirteenth, false accusation; at the fourteenth, tale-bearing; at the fifteenth, delight-and-discontent; at the sixteenth, slander of others; at the seventeenth, deceitful falsehood; at the eighteenth, the thorn of wrong belief — among all these eighteen sources of sin, whichever source of sin my soul has committed, has had committed, or has approved while another committed it, all that, by me, by mind, by speech, by body — may my misdeed come to nothing.
idiomaticTranslation: First, violence; second, lying; third, theft; fourth, unchastity; fifth, possessiveness; sixth, anger; seventh, pride; eighth, deceit; ninth, greed; tenth, attachment; eleventh, aversion; twelfth, strife; thirteenth, false accusation; fourteenth, backbiting; fifteenth, craving-and-loathing; sixteenth, slander; seventeenth, cunning falsehood; and eighteenth, the thorn of wrong belief. Of all these eighteen seats of sin, whatever fault my soul has done itself, prompted in another, or applauded in another's doing — every bit of it, with my mind, my speech and my body, I now disown: may my misdeed come to nothing.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Read closely, the eighteen are not a random heap but a descent inward and then a return outward. The verse opens with the five great restraints in their canonical order — prāṇātipāta, mṛṣāvāda, adattādāna, maithuna, parigraha — gross acts of body and word. It then turns to the four kaṣāyas (krodha, māna, māyā, lobha, items 6–9), the inner passions that drive those acts, before naming their very engine, rāga and dveṣa (10–11). Items 12–17 are the social and verbal harms that rāga–dveṣa breed: quarrelling (kalaha), false accusation (abhyākhyāna), tale-bearing (cāḍī), the restless rati-arati, slander (para-parivāda), and cunning falsehood (māyā-mṛṣāvāda). The list culminates not in another act but in mithyātva-śalya — wrong belief named as a *śalya*, an embedded thorn: the deepest source, since a distorted view of reality silently feeds all seventeen above it. A deliberate hinge follows the enumeration: 'e aḍhāre… jiko koi… māre jīve' — 'of all eighteen, whichever my own soul has touched.' The confession is not abstract; it is pinned to the reciter's own jīva. The triad sevyuṁ–sevarāvyuṁ–anumodyuṁ then closes every escape: one is implicated whether one acted, set another to act, or merely smiled on the act. 'Sevatāṁ pratye anumodyuṁ' is the subtle clause — quiet approval of another's wrongdoing is itself confessed. Finally 'huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī' binds the three instruments, and 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' seals it. The grammar of the verse thus enacts its doctrine: name exhaustively, locate honestly, retract completely.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse's distinctive teaching is its ranking of sin by depth, not merely by gravity. By placing mithyātva-śalya last and calling it a thorn, it asserts the Jain conviction that wrong darśana (view) is the taproot of karmic influx — the five gross sins and four passions are its branches. This mirrors the Tattvārtha framing in which mithyādarśana heads the causes of bandha and right faith (samyag-darśana) is the first step of the path. Equally doctrinal is the pairing rāga–dveṣa as the twin axle of saṁsāra: every kaṣāya resolves into one or the other. The verse therefore offers a compact map of āsrava — from view, through passion, to deed and word.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter today can use the eighteen as a graded conscience-scan: run down the list, pause where it stings, and apply the sevyuṁ / sevarāvyuṁ / anumodyuṁ test honestly — especially the third, since approving harmful talk, sharp business, or another's slander is the fault most easily overlooked. Reaching mithyātva-śalya last is a reminder to examine not only what one did but how one sees, before letting 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' genuinely loosen the thorn.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha Sūtra 8.1 — mithyādarśana, avirati, pramāda, kaṣāya and yoga as the causes of karmic bondage
crossReferences.source: Umāsvāti, tr. Nathmal Tatia, That Which Is

crossReferences.text: Sthānāṅga / Samavāyāṅga — canonical enumeration of the eighteen pāpasthānaka
Āvaśyaka tradition — the Pratikramaṇa confession formula sevita-kārita-anumodita

sources.title: Micchami Dukkadam
sources.consultedFor: Meaning and Prakrit/Sanskrit etymology of micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ and its role in the pratikramaṇa confession.

sources.title: Karma in Jainism
sources.consultedFor: Doctrinal framing of āsrava and bandha, kaṣāya and rāga–dveṣa as causes of karmic influx.

sources.title: Tattvartha Sutra
sources.consultedFor: Placement of mithyādarśana as the foremost cause of bondage, grounding the verse's culmination in mithyātva-śalya.

notes: Item 14 is recited as the Gujarati 'cāḍī' (tale-bearing), corresponding to classical paiśunya; rendered accordingly. The ordinal locatives (pahele, bīje…) are Old/medieval Gujarati liturgical forms meaning 'at the Nth (place)'; transliterated as pronounced. 'jiko' is an archaic relative pronoun ('whichever'). 'māre jīve' read as instrumental 'by my soul'. The triad sevyuṁ/sevarāvyuṁ/anumodyuṁ maps to kṛta/kārita/anumodita and mane/vacane/kāyāe to the three yogas — explained per-token rather than re-glossing the preface's grid. Source spelling preserved verbatim (e.g. અતિયાર-style readings not at issue in this verse). Variant enumerations exist for items 16–18 across sects, but the eighteen-fold total and content are stable; the recited order here is followed exactly.
